titlestmt.titleproper: Ernst Curtius and Ernst Robert Curtius correspondence6957
titlestmt.author: Compiled by Laurent Ferri

publicationstmt.publisher: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2021-08-22 18:39:28 UTC.

change.item: This resource was modified by the ArchivesSpace Preprocessor developed by the Harvard Library (https://github.com/harvard-library/archivesspace-preprocessor)

repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.unittitle: Ernst Curtius and Ernst Robert Curtius correspondence
did.abstract: Letters to Ernst Curtius and Ernst Robert Curtius, covering the period 1841-1956
did.langmaterial: Collection material in English, French, and German.

defitem.label: Contact Information:
defitem.item: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections 2B Carl A. Kroch Library Cornell University Ithaca, NY 14853 (607) 255-3530 Fax: (607) 255-9524 rareref@cornell.edu http://rmc.library.cornell.edu

bioghist.head: BIOGRAPHICAL NOTE
bioghist.p: Ernst Curtius (1814-1896) was an eminent philologist and archeologist. From 1844-50 he served as tutor ("Zivilgouverneur") to Prince Friedrich von Hohenzollern, afterwards Emperor Friedrich III. He was also professor of classical philology, archeology, and eloquence at the Universities of Göttingen and Berlin. In 1874 Curtius concluded an agreement with the Greek authorities by which the excavations in Olympia were entrusted exclusively to the Germans. What he found there ultimately led to the re-institution of the Olympic Games by Baron Pierre de Coubertin in 1894.

His grandson Ernst Robert Curtius (1886-1956) was a foremost literary scholar of the 20th century. The son of high-ranking civil servant, he grew up in Alsace, a French province annexed by the Reich between 1871 and 1918, and was thus bilingual in German and French -- and also fluent in English, Italian, and Spanish. Under the aegis of Strasbourg Professor Gustav Gröber, he devoted his doctoral dissertation to editing an Old French epic, but his "Habilitationsschrift" in Bonn was on the contemporary French critique Ferdinand Brunetière: thus emerged the two paths, i.e. the study of "medieval literature" and the critical appraisal of recent works, that he was to follow throughout his life. Curtius rejected Brunetière's "scientific" method, instead favoring intuition, attention to nuances, and "elected affinities" as the most meaningful avenues toward literary knowledge. After being mobilized and wounded in Poland during World War I, he obtained a tenured position at the University of Bonn in 1916. Eevn though he considered the politicization of spiritual and intellectual life as a betrayal of the truly independent thinker, his scholarship in the 1920s often seemed to support a political, pan-European agenda: "Der Europagedanke, he would say, mußte geistig gebaut werden. Dazu wollten meine Bücher helfen." Curtius took part in international symposiums in Pontigny (Burgundy) and Colpach (Luxemburg); was in touch with the Austrian prince von Rohan (who had launched an "Europäische Revue" in 1925, and would join join the Nazi Party in 1938); and corresponded with Parisian intellectuals of all stripes, despite the divisions generated by two wars (1870-71, 1914-18). The exchange slowed down considerably after 1933 however, and not only because of the passing of his beloved Catherine Pozzi (1882-1934), a poetess and former lover of Paul Valéry who wrote scientific articles and translated the poetry of Stefan George. Curtius dedicated to her his essay, "James Joyce's Ulysses" (1929) and also corresponded with Pozzi's son, Claude Bourdet, a young engineer who would become a major figure of the Resistance and create the weekly "L'Observateur" (after 1964 "Le Nouvel Observateur.") In 1931, Catherine Pozzi wrote to Curtius: "In Frankreich scheint das Geistige noch in keiner Gefahr zu stehn, wie bei Ihnen." Indeed, with Hitler leading the new Reich, life became almost impossible for German intellectuals. A fervent patriot and a Stoic, Curtius decided to stay in Germany and to focus on medieval and Renaissance literature, where less political interference would be encountered. Despite his commitment to "European unity" he would reject any "collaboration" between Germany and France after 1940 -- to the disappointment of his admirer Jacques Benoist-Méchin (1901-1983), a brilliant journalist, military historian, and Proust scholar who occupied a number of high positions in the Vichy regime. In 1950, back from the Institute for Advanced Studies at Princeton, Curtius wrote to Anne Heurgon-Desjardins: "Les querelles franco-allemandes m'ennuient. Je considère ces deux peuples comme des collégiens mal élevés qui se chamaillent. J'ai fait métier de rapprocheur pendant vingt ans. J'ai maintenant droit à la retraite."

relatedmaterial.head: RELATED MATERIALS
relatedmaterial.p: "Deutsch-französiche Gespräche, 1920-1950. La Correspondance de Ernst Robert Curtius avec André Gide, Charles du Bos et Valery Larbaud" Herbert et Jane M. Dieckmann ed., Frankfurt am Main: Vittorio Klostermann, 1980.

prefercite.head: Cite As:
prefercite.p: Letters to Ernst Curtius and Ernst Robert Curtius, #6957. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library. There is no restriction in terms of access.

acqinfo.head: PROVENANCE
acqinfo.p: The letters were given to the Division of Rare Books and Manuscripts in Kroch Library in 2001 by Mrs. Jane Marsh Dieckmann, widow of Herbert Dieckmann (1906-1986), a professor of Romance Studies at Harvard (1950-66) and Cornell (1966-74.) Born in North Rhine-Westphalia, Herbert Dieckmann wrote his doctorate in Bonn under the direction of Ernst Robert Curtius, and left Germany in 1933. A specialist of Diderot, he spent his last years co-editing and annotating the voluminous correspondence between his former professor and André Gide, Valéry Larbaud, and Charles Du Bos. Letters from other European intellectuals and writers were handed over to the Dieckmann by Ilse Curtius, but not selected for publication -- they form the present collection.

scopecontent.head: Scope and content
scopecontent.p: This collection contains about a hundred letters and some drawings, addressed mostly by Parisian intellectuals, poets and scholars to Ernst Curtius and Ernst Robert Curtius, two prominent members of the German educated upper middle-class (Bildungsbürgertum), between 1841 and 1934. Correspondents include Sylvia Beach, Jean Cocteau, Stefan George, André Gide, James Joyce, Thomas Mann, Marcel Proust, Rainer Maria Rilke, Paul Valéry, and others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and content
scopecontent.p: Also included is a letter from Ernst Curtius to Theodor Heinrich Travers (November 14, 1845).

did.unittitle: Series 1 : Letters from and to Ernst Curtius
Hase, Karl Benedict [Curator at the Département des Manuscrits of the Bibliothèque nationale in Paris], to "Monsieur Curtius, Professeur de littérature ancienne à l'Université Royale de Berlin"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in German: "Wir gehen einer Zeit entgegen, wo es von der größten Wichtigkeit sein wird, daß der Beherrscher des einzigen waffenmächtigen protestantischen Staates in Europa (England ist keine Landmacht) teutschen Sinn habe, klug und fest sei, gleich entfernt von Gleichgültigkeit für das Höchste und von befangener Frömmelei. Und kann irgend etwas in Ihrem Zögling solche Gefühle wecken, solche Eigenschaften entwickeln, so sind es gewiß Ihre Lehren und Ihr Beispiel.".

did.unittitle: Hase, Karl Benedict [Curator at the Département des Manuscrits of the Bibliothèque nationale in Paris], to "Monsieur Curtius, Professeur de littérature ancienne à l'Université Royale de Berlin"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in German: "Bedenken Sie, verehrtester Freund, wie wichtig es für das Wohl eines ganzen Landes ist dass dessen künftiger Herrscher nicht bloß Hofstaat und Hofprediger um sich habe, sondern auch Männer voll Geist, Wissenschaft und Verstand, teutsch gesinnt, vertraut mit den großen Alten."

did.unittitle: Curtius, Ernst, to Theodor Heinrich Travers

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in German about his position as tutor and preceptor of Kronprinz Friedrich Wilhelm: "Der Prinz entwickelt sich körperlich und geistig zu seinem Vortheile, sein Umgang wird auch für mich immer angenehmer, meine Tätigkeit immer geistiger und würdiger; er geht in sein 13. Jahr, ist aber von meiner Größe, stark und gesund. Im Sommer besuchte ich mit ihm meine Heimat [Lübeck], dann lebten wir auf dem schönen Babelsberger Schlosse [...] bis um Ende Oktober"; also about his academic and social life in Berlin ("Berlin ist jetzt ein ganz anderer Ort geworden, statt des früheren flauen Indifferentismus in allen öffentlichen Dingen ist es jetzt der Ort lebendigen Bewegungen geworden, die durch das ganze Reich pulsieren"); about his friendship with playwright and librettist Emanuel Geibel (1815-1884): "Auch lese ich wieder an der Universität. [...] Dieser Augenblick ist auch Geibel wieder hier, körperlich sehr leidend, aber innerlich sehr produktiv; er schreibt u.A. auch eine Oper jetzt für Mendelssohn [...] Von seinen Gedichten wird die 5. Auflage gedruckt; er steht recht gut"; finally, about his travel projects: "Ich habe entschieden die Absicht, nach Beendigung meiner jetzigen Stellung -- kleine 3 1/2 Jahre -- eine Reise nach Italien und Griechenland zu machen."

did.unittitle: Laborde, Léon de [Curator of the Départment de Antiquités at the Louvre], to Ernst Curtius

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

did.unittitle: Luynes, Honoré d'Albert de [Archeologist and Numismatist], to Ernst Curtius

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

did.unittitle: Rochette, Raoul [Curator at the Cabinet des Médailles of the Bibliothèque nationale in Paris]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French related to his being introduced to Prince Friedrich of Prussia.

did.unittitle: Rochette, Raoul [Curator at the Cabinet des Médailles of the Bibliothèque nationale in Paris]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: "Vous êtes, Monsieur, du petit nombre des philologues allemands qui joignent à l'étude de la langue, base essentielle de toute connaissance sérieuse de l'Antiquité, le goût et l'intelligence des monuments."

did.unittitle: Series 2: Letters to Ernst Robert Curtius

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in German about the poor reception of his poetry in France: "Ich schwöre Ihnen, [dass] kein französicher Kritik, dem ich mein Buch gesendet habe, mir ein einfaches Dankwort geschrieben hat". Offers to send Curtius a copy of "Feu de Joie": "Ich würde zufrieden sein, es Ihnen [zu] senden. Hier aber, hielt man es für unverständliches Pathos, und dafür wage ich nicht selbt diese Jugend- (was sage ich?), [diese] Kindheit-Gedichte jemandem zu geben."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French ("Je crois, Monsieur, que bous ne goûtez guère mes manières de lycéen avec la langue allemande") about his detestation of "literary nationalism."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in Spanish.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in English, about James Joyce (his recent books and problems with his eyes.)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in English, about James Joyce (his recent books and problems with his eyes.)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French. B-M. offers to meet in Marburg, rather than France; he's ashamed of the decadence of his country, doesn't want to wear the French uniform when they meet.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French about Claudel, Valéry, Proust. "France can still produce men like Valéry, but not a Rimbaud or a Lenin."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: "La France est un vieux pays pourri de littérature et de bourgeoisie"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French ("Vous conservez un héritage de piété et de dignité intellectuelles... Le traîner jusqu'à l'autre rive peut-il excéder vos forces, si vous savez que sur l'autre rive, tant d'êtres vous attendent et vous saluent?")

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French. Mostly about Marcel Proust.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French. About literary critique, Gide, Du Bos...

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in German, written after the passing of his mother [Catherine Pozzi]: "Ja, wir werden Freunde bleiben."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in German, about publishing Catherine Pozzi's poem "Peau d'âme" and the situation in Europe ("dass die Flucht von Hass, von Dummheit und Kulturvernichtung sich nähert, dass sie fast schon da ist, quelle tristesse!")

did.unittitle: Brémond, Abbé Henri, to E.R. Curtius

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

did.unittitle: Brémond, Abbé Henri, to E.R. Curtius

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French, annotated by Curtius in German: "von Brunschvigg [sic], über "Essai sur la France" aufheben!"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French, about the death of Radiguet and the alleged misperception of his work in France ("La France qui juge vite me croit saltimbanque.")

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Telegram in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French, mostly about "the Americanization of Europe."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French, mostly about his writing projects.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: writing projects; Paul Éluard reads Proust.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: "J'aime vos compliments, mais pas ce que vous dites de la Révolution... Notre capitalisme, nos nationalisme, le ridicule pan-européanisme à la Coudenhove-Kalergi ont fait leur preuve. Votre Hitler, notre Action Française se valent -- ne valent pas cher. Si vous avez une minute, dites-moi pourquoi vous traitez ainsi la Révolution. Je ne crois pas au libéralisme."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

did.unittitle: Evrard, Eugène, Jacques, to E.R. Curtius

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

did.unittitle: Friess, Ruth [wife of Horace L. Friess], to E.R. Curtius

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in English.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: a friendly arranging to meet Curtius in Paris; he will not see him at the gathering arranged by "Charlie D.B." [Charles Du Bos], because he will be in Cuverville for his wedding anniversary ; he therefore invites Curtius and his wife to have tea on another day, rue Vaneau, with "Mme Théo [van Rysselberghe]". Gide asks Curtius to give him a phone call "in the morning before 11AM."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: the practice of philosophy, the disciples, Thomas Aquinas...

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Dante, Barrès...

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French, about "L'Essai sur la France."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French, about "L'Essai sur la France."

did.unittitle: Nathan, Roger ["L'Europe Nouvelle"], to E.R. Curtius

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French, about "L'Essai sur la France."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French, about the concept of European culture.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French, about the concept of European culture.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French, about her literary career ("Je n'ai pas de salon, parce que je n'ai pas de diplomatie.") and her interest in the sciences.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French, about languages, the process of learning...

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French, about creation, dream, the universe...

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: New Year greetings, their friendship...

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: their friendship, Rome and Curtius as "a Roman" ("Adieu, tu, Romane!").

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: invitation to go together in Dordogne; the relative importance of France and Germany ("On voit très bien que toutes les aventures spirituelles les plus hardies et les plus gênantes pour la collectivité paraissent partir d'Allemagne... Luther est allemand. Nietzsche est allemand . Cette sorte de volonté d'atteindre quelque chose qui transcende, et de l'atteindre envers et contre tout, et de détruire pour cela, s'il le faut, tout le reste, c'est allemand.")

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: personalism, freedom...

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French, containing fragments of a poetic essay.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French and German: their literary friendship.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Prose poem in German.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: her social status and social obligations in Paris.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French, with a photographic portrait of C. Pozzi and Paul Valéry, and by two original watercolors by C. Pozzi, showing her country house in Provence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Curtius's "Balzac," the "scientific nature" of his work ; with a picture of C. Pozzi as "Séraphitus."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French about her social life in Paris.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: philosophical considerations.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Hofmannstahl; the political opinions of her son Claude Bourdet ("J'ai un fils de dix-neuf ans. Il est très jeune-français d'aujourd'hui; assez américanisé... travaillant à Zürich et donc en allemand... d'Action Française avec passion... C'est un fait que la jeunesse va en masse à l'A.F. ou au communisme") and her own ("Le rapprochement franco-allemand ne se fera pas par la République [mais] par une politique de droite et par une diplomatie d'Ancien régime.")

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French and English: Baden; her mood and feelings.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: the nature of their friendship.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Tapuscript in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: "L'écriture, c'est l'état le moins acceptable aux techniciens [...] Oui, c'est l'état [de] pythie."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French, "envoyé des hyper-mondes" (sic).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: poems in French, German, and Italian.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: nature, science, plus corrections on a tatuscript.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: sonnet addressed by Curtius to C. Pozzi, with her follow-up in verses.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: flowers and insects.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French about Maurice Barrès.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: about the possibility of translating poems by Stefan George (at Paulhan's request.)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Paulhan's "betrayal."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: René Schwob, Charles Du Bos...

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Curtius's next sojourn in France.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Curtius's marriage; André Siegfried, Edouard Bourdet, Maurice Barrès.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Curtius's marriage; Gide.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: their friendship, journalism.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French about her readings and correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Proust, Bernard Groethuysen.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: literary honors ("On ne donnerait plus le Prix Nobel à Dante.")

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: review of Curtius's book in "Le Figaro." With an undated postcard on the same subject.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French, containing a handwritten poem by her, "Nova."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French (postcard from Vence.)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Edouard Bourdet, James Joyce, France-Allemagne.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: annotated and corrected proofs for "Un Manuel de civilisation française."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Claude Bourdet and the International Congress of Students in Paris.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Europe, China.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Paulhan's decision not to hire her as scientific journalist for the "NRF."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Herrad von Landsberg.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: reception of Curtius in France.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Sieburg's "Dieu est-il français ?" (Grasset, 1930)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Sieburg's "Dieu est-il français ?" (Grasset, 1930); project of an interview of André Siegfried titled "Contre l'Amérique!"; France and Germany.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: health problems; Vence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: body and soul; Diderot and Montaigne.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French about Gnosticism and Christianism ("Nous sommes catholiques, ER!")

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: the spirit of the Middle Ages and the present time.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: news of her son Claude; her readings: Racine, Corneille.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French and English. With a poem in German, "Todwasser," composed by her son Claude Bourdet.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in German and French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in German: vacations in Vence and in the Alps.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French and German: "Comment ne verrais-je pas l'Allemagne à travers vous? Elle vous a fait. Sa jeunesse vous lit et vous écoute..."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: writings and readings (Jules Romains, Stefan George); friendship with Marie Kiener; her Italian origins.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: her translations of Lucretius.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: relation of a dinner with the Nietzsche scholar [and Nazi sympathiser] Friedrich Würzbach and René Schwob, "le Boswell de Gide."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: St Paul, Maritain...

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Tibullus, Colette...

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: André Siegfried, Italy...

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French about metaphysics.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: philosophy and science.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: her writings and interest in science.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: the death of her mother.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: "Suivre votre travail aura été le dernier plaisir d'un certain moi appliqué au monde."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: her travels in Europe.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: despair.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French and German: her property in Dordogne.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in German.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Proust, Benda...

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: the task of the cleric, the importance of erudition...

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: chronology and style.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in German: news from the house, her son...

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in German: considerations about life, the animals...

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French ("Vous voulez que je vous écrive en français"): her mood and writings.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: their correspondence; Curtius's "Balzac."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French and ancient Greek: her friends in Paris (Abel Bonnard, Jacques Maritain); the correspondence with Rilke.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: "Je vais entreprendre de vous écrire avec un stylo cassé." Annotated by Curtius: "letzter Brief."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Balzac, Stendhal, Valéry; Curtius's "Essai sur la France."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: condolences.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French about Curtius's essay on Balzac: "Vous avez fait de la critique créatrice, celle qui s'impose quand on a à parler d'un créateur comme Balzac."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: Bergson; Décade de Pontigny.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: sojourns of young French intellectuals (incl. Armand Bérard) in Heidelberg, Germany, at Curtius's invitation.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French: notions of literary critique.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French about their respective publications.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: ALS in French. Responding to a pressing request made by Curtius to employ German scholars who would otherwise be facing persecution by the Nazi regime, Paul Valéry explains why, in his capacity as the general administrator of the "Centre Universitaire Méditerranéen" in Nice, he is unfortunately unable to do anything : his authority is limited by government regulations as well as by financial constraints; an academic center is not a university, and therefore can offer only lectures to visiting faculty, not regular teaching; there are no professorial chairs, unless foreign governments wish to endow them, etc.
